passage: The community garden behind the library opened in early May. Each plot was about ten feet square. Most of them were planted with tomatoes, peppers, and herbs by the end of the month.

questions.q: What was planted in most plots?

passage: Howard had been baking sourdough bread for nearly thirty years. His starter, a cup of bubbly fermented dough, had been with him for the entire stretch. He fed it flour and water every day, like an old pet.

questions.q: How long has Howard been baking sourdough?
What does he do for the starter every day?

passage: The thunderstorm came up suddenly in the afternoon. Lightning lit the dark clouds and the rain fell in sheets. Within twenty minutes the storm had passed, leaving a fresh-smelling breeze and small pools of water along the road.

questions.q: How long did the storm last?
What was left along the road?
What happened to the breeze afterwards?

passage: The weekly farmers' market on Main Street drew a steady crowd by mid-morning. Vendors sold fresh vegetables, jams, and homemade bread. A small band played in the corner near the bandstand.

questions.q: When did the crowd build up?
Name two things the vendors sold.

passage: Cleo, the gray tabby, had a routine. She greeted the morning at the back door, ate her breakfast, and then climbed up to the windowsill to watch the birds. By noon she was asleep on the couch.

questions.q: What kind of cat is Cleo?
Where does she sleep by noon?
What does she watch from the windowsill?

passage: The roof of the old shed was starting to leak. Stewart climbed up with a tarp and laid it across the worst spot. He would call a roofer in the morning to give an estimate.

questions.q: What was wrong with the shed?
What did Stewart use to cover the leak?

questions.a: Call a roofer for an estimate

passage: Mr. Donato has run the corner grocery for forty years. He knows every customer by name and remembers what they like. The cashier his daughter trained last summer is now learning to do the same.

questions.q: How long has Mr. Donato run the grocery?
What does he remember about his customers?

passage: The pumpkin patch opens the first weekend of October. Families come to pick their own pumpkins from the field. There are also hay rides, hot apple cider, and a small barn with goats and sheep.

questions.q: When does the pumpkin patch open?
Name two things you can do besides pick pumpkins.
questions.a: Hay rides, hot apple cider, or a barn with goats and sheep

passage: Helen's letter to her grandson took most of the morning. She wanted to tell him about the new puppy and the trip to the lake. By the time she sealed the envelope she had filled three pages.

questions.q: What did she want to tell him about?
questions.a: The new puppy and the trip to the lake

passage: The garage was full of boxes that needed to be sorted. Some held tools, some held photographs, and a few were filled with old magazines. Carl gave himself one Saturday a month to work on it.

questions.q: What did Carl find in the boxes?
How often did he work on the garage?

passage: The coffee shop on the corner was busy every weekday morning. Workers came in for cups before the train at quarter past eight. By nine the rush was over and the café grew quiet.

questions.q: When was the coffee shop busy?
What time did the train leave?

passage: The river ran along the bottom of the meadow. In summer it was shallow enough to walk across. After heavy rain it could swell up, brown and quick, and you had to take the bridge instead.

questions.a: Along the bottom of the meadow

questions.q: What had to be done after heavy rain?

passage: The recipe for the apple cake had been in the family for three generations. Vivian's grandmother had brought it from Vienna. Now Vivian made it every fall when the apples came down from the orchard.

questions.q: Where did the recipe come from?

passage: The wood stove in the cabin needed kindling and a few small logs. Once the fire caught, the room warmed in less than an hour. By bedtime the cabin was cozy and the windows had fogged up against the cold.

questions.q: How long did it take for the room to warm?
What had fogged up by bedtime?

passage: Every morning the mail carrier walked the same route. She knew which dogs to watch out for and which porches had treats. The job took her about four hours, even in good weather.

questions.q: How long did the mail route take?
What did she watch out for?

passage: The old quilt on the bed was made by hand. Each square was cut from a different shirt or dress in the family. Some pieces were forty years old, some were newer, and the colors had faded to a soft, even blue.

questions.q: What were the squares cut from?
questions.a: Old shirts and dresses in the family

questions.q: How old were the oldest pieces?
What color had they faded to?

passage: Bowling night was every Thursday at seven o'clock. Bert had been on the same team for over twenty years. The team had won the league trophy three times and finished second more often than that.

questions.q: How long has Bert been on his team?
How many times did they win the trophy?

passage: The tomato plants needed staking by the end of June. Otherwise they would topple under the weight of the fruit. Jack used wooden stakes from the shed and tied each plant up with strips of old cloth.

questions.q: When did the plants need staking?

questions.a: So they wouldn't topple under the weight of the fruit

questions.q: What did Jack use to tie them?

passage: There was a small antique shop on Elm Street. Marie liked to stop in on Saturday afternoons. She rarely bought anything but enjoyed looking at the old clocks, china teacups, and silver spoons.

questions.q: Name two things she likes to look at.
questions.a: Old clocks, china teacups, or silver spoons

passage: The blueberry bushes by the back fence had been there for over thirty years. They produced a heavy crop each July. Most of the berries went into pies, jam, and the freezer.

questions.q: How long had the bushes been there?
What was made from the berries?

passage: The old upright piano in the living room had not been tuned in years. Margaret arranged for a tuner to come on a Tuesday afternoon. He spent two hours adjusting the strings before he was satisfied.
The fence around the yard was weathered. Each year a few boards had to be replaced. Roger picked up the lumber on Saturday morning and finished the work by lunchtime.

questions.q: What was wrong with the fence?
questions.a: Weathered, and a few boards needed replacing

questions.q: When did Roger pick up the lumber?

passage: The bookstore on Maple Avenue had a small reading section in the back. Comfortable chairs sat between the shelves. Customers were welcome to sit and read for as long as they liked.
Sheila's vegetable garden produced more zucchini than her family could eat. She gave a basket to her neighbor every week in August. The neighbor returned the kindness with jars of homemade pickles.

questions.q: What did the garden produce too much of?
What did Sheila give the neighbor?
questions.a: A basket of zucchini every week

questions.q: What did the neighbor give back?

passage: The trail through the woods was about three miles long. It looped around the old reservoir and back to the parking lot. Most days it took just over an hour to walk.

questions.q: How long does it take to walk?

passage: The snow finally melted in early April. The first crocuses came up by the porch a week later. Then came the daffodils, in clumps near the front walk.

questions.a: In clumps near the front walk

passage: The boiler in the basement clanked when it started up. It was an old unit and it had its quirks. Wendell had a heating company come in to check it before every winter.

questions.q: What does it do when it starts?
When does Wendell have it checked?

passage: The Sunday paper was twice as thick as the weekday edition. Maureen liked to read it slowly with a second cup of coffee. The crossword usually took her until lunchtime.

questions.q: How thick is the Sunday paper?
questions.a: Twice as thick as the weekday edition

questions.q: How long did the crossword take?

passage: The bird bath in the back garden filled with leaves every autumn. Chuck cleaned it out once a week and topped it up with fresh water. The robins and the cardinals came back as soon as it was clean.

questions.q: What filled the bird bath in autumn?
How often did Chuck clean it?
What kinds of birds came back?

passage: The walk to the bus stop was three blocks. Patsy timed it so she would be there exactly five minutes before the bus arrived. She used the time to chat with whoever else was waiting.

questions.q: How far is the bus stop?
When did Patsy try to arrive?
What did she do with the time?

passage: The bookcase in the den held a row of photo albums. Each was labeled with a year. Sometimes Carlos pulled one down at random to look through old pictures of his children.

The sewing machine in the corner had belonged to her mother. Lila had used it to make curtains, dresses, and even a few quilts over the years. The motor still hummed evenly after almost sixty years of use.

questions.q: Name two things Lila made on it.

passage: The neighborhood barbecue happened on the Saturday before the Fourth of July. Each family brought a dish to share. The Petersons were known for their potato salad and Mr. Vega for his ribs.

questions.a: The Saturday before the Fourth of July

questions.q: What was Mr. Vega known for?

passage: The wooden rocking chair on the porch had been refinished twice. The cushion was an old quilted square stuffed with batting. Lewis liked to read there in the late afternoon when the light was gold.

questions.q: How many times had the chair been refinished?
What was the cushion stuffed with?
When did Lewis like to read there?

passage: The lumberyard was always busy on Saturdays. Contractors and homeowners loaded their trucks with two-by-fours, plywood, and cement bags. Theo worked the counter and knew most of the regulars.
The herbs in the kitchen window grew well. Basil, parsley, and mint took up three small pots on the sill. Dolores snipped a sprig or two whenever she was cooking.

The clock repair shop on Pine Street had been there for over fifty years. The owner, Mr. Owens, could fix watches, mantel clocks, and grandfather clocks. He always had a few projects laid out on his bench.

questions.q: How long has it been there?
Name two things Mr. Owens repairs.
questions.a: Watches, mantel clocks, or grandfather clocks

passage: The diner on the highway never closed. Truckers stopped in at all hours, and the waitresses were used to the night shift. The pie case held a fresh selection every morning, baked on the premises.

questions.q: How often does the diner close?

passage: The lake froze over by January and stayed solid until late March. People drilled holes in the ice and fished for perch. There were even a few small huts set up where the regulars could stay warm.

The hedge along the driveway needed trimming. Carl pulled out the old electric trimmer and ran an extension cord from the garage. By the end of the afternoon there was a neat line all the way to the street.

questions.q: Where did the extension cord run from?

questions.a: By the end of the afternoon

passage: Claude collected stamps as a hobby. He kept them in a thick book with plastic sleeves on every page. Some of the stamps were nearly a hundred years old and came from countries that no longer existed.

questions.a: In a thick book with plastic sleeves

passage: The grocery store on Sunday morning was nearly empty. Joan liked to shop early, before the after-church crowd. She had her cart filled and was at the register by nine-fifteen.

questions.q: When did Joan like to shop?
When was she at the register?

passage: The sewing circle met on the second Wednesday of each month. Each member brought their current project and a snack. Over coffee and cookies they would share progress and offer tips.

questions.a: The second Wednesday of each month
A current project and a snack

passage: The corner of the basement had been turned into a workshop. There was a workbench, a pegboard for tools, and a small radio. Doug spent winter afternoons there building birdhouses for the spring.

questions.a: A workbench, a pegboard for tools, and a small radio

passage: The little market by the train station kept long hours. It opened at six in the morning and closed at midnight. Pearl stopped in once a week for milk, fruit, and a paper.
The blackberry bushes along the back lane were heavy with fruit. Theo took a small bucket out and filled it in less than an hour. The berries went into a pie crust for that night's dessert.

questions.q: How long did it take to fill the bucket?
What did Theo make with the berries?

passage: The old town hall had a brass bell that still rang on the hour. It could be heard four blocks away on a quiet day. Visitors often asked about it, and there was a small plaque inside explaining its history.

questions.q: How far away could it be heard?

passage: The garden shed needed a new coat of paint. Eli scraped off the loose flakes, sanded the rough patches, and laid down two coats of fresh white. By the next weekend the shed looked almost new.

questions.q: How many coats of paint did he apply?

passage: The community center offered free yoga classes on Tuesday mornings. About a dozen people showed up most weeks. The instructor brought extra mats for anyone who didn't have one.
The old fishing rod in the basement had belonged to Mike's father. The reel still worked smoothly after all these years. Mike planned to take his grandson out to the pond and teach him to cast it.

questions.q: Where had the rod been kept?
What did Mike plan to do?
questions.a: Take his grandson to the pond and teach him to cast
